_meta.description: Detailed signage rules for Quebec businesses — French language requirements, sign permit rules, and Montreal-specific bylaws. Companion to bill96.json.

plain_language_summary: If your business has any public-facing sign — storefront, market stall banner, vehicle wrap, window decal — it must comply with Quebec's French language rules (Bill 96) AND Montreal's municipal sign permit rules. Most small violations result in a warning first, but repeat offenders face fines. Getting this right from day one is easy and cheap.

french_language_rules.core_rule: French must be markedly predominant on all commercial public signage in Quebec
what_markedly_predominant_means: French text must be at least twice the size of any other language text
French must appear first (above or to the left of other languages)
French must be at least as visible in terms of contrast and placement
The overall impression of the sign must be that French dominates
applies_to: Price tags and shelf labels in retail
does_not_apply_to: Internal signage not visible from outside (though French service must still be offered)
Signs in private spaces with no public access

business_name_rules.french_name: If your business name is in French — fully compliant as-is
business_name_rules.english_or_other_name: If your business name is in English or another language, you have two options: (1) Add a French descriptor or slogan that appears in markedly larger text, or (2) Redesign the name to incorporate French elements

examples.name: Marcus Tech Solutions
examples.fix: Add French descriptor e.g. 'Solutions technologiques' in larger text below

french_language_rules.generic_descriptors_exception: Recognized trademarks and brand names have some protection — the OQLF has specific rules for franchises and registered trademarks. Contact OQLF for your specific situation.

montreal_sign_permits.plain_language: Beyond language rules, Montreal requires permits for certain types of signs. Small signs on your storefront window usually don't need a permit. Large or illuminated signs usually do.
no_permit_required_typically: Window decals under a certain size threshold (verify with borough)
A-frame / sandwich board signs on your own property during business hours
permit_required_typically: Any sign attached to the exterior of a building
Projecting signs (extends perpendicular from the building)
Signs over a certain square footage (threshold varies by borough and zone)
Signs on vehicles permanently parked as advertising
montreal_sign_permits.how_to_apply: Contact your borough office. Bring: proposed sign dimensions, design/mockup, location on building, materials.
montreal_sign_permits.cost: Typically $100–$300 for standard sign permit — varies by borough and sign type

home_based_no_storefront.signage_needs: Minimal — you may want a small identification sign on your door or mailbox
home_based_no_storefront.french_requirement: Even a small nameplate should be in French or bilingual with French predominant
home_based_no_storefront.permit: Usually not required for a small residential identification plaque — verify with borough
home_based_no_storefront.tip: Many home-based businesses skip physical signage entirely and focus on online presence

market_vendor.french_requirement: All market signage must be French-predominant. Price tags in French.
market_vendor.permit: No city permit needed for temporary market stall signs — but market organizer may have their own rules
market_vendor.tip: Invest in a good bilingual banner — it's your main marketing at the market. French on top/larger, English below/smaller.

commercial_storefront.signage_needs: Exterior sign, window signage, interior menu/price boards
commercial_storefront.french_requirement: Full compliance required — all exterior signage French-predominant
commercial_storefront.permit: Required for most exterior signs — apply to borough before installing
commercial_storefront.tip: Get the sign permit BEFORE you order the sign — permits can take 2-4 weeks and you don't want to be stuck with a non-compliant sign

enforcement.who: Office québécois de la langue française (OQLF) for language compliance; borough inspectors for sign permits
enforcement.process: Complaint-based and periodic inspections. OQLF typically sends a compliance notice first before fining.
enforcement.first_violation: Usually a written notice with 90 days to comply
enforcement.fines: Up to $20,000 for a business; up to $700 for an individual
enforcement.montreal_sign_permit_fines: Operating without a required sign permit: $500–$2,000

practical_tips: When in doubt, make your sign predominantly French and add other languages in smaller text — you'll never be wrong
Keep proof that you applied for sign permits even if still pending — shows good faith
OQLF has a free consultation service — call them before installing signage if unsure
Many print shops in Montreal are familiar with Bill 96 requirements and can advise

free_consultation.note: OQLF offers free guidance for businesses trying to comply — they prefer helping you get it right over fining you

government_body: Office québécois de la langue française (OQLF)
